references.title: What is wrong with deep knowledge tracing? Attention-based knowledge tracing

extracted.Students: 4417 ASSISTments09 learners, 19917 ASSISTments15 learners, 686 ASSISTmentsChall learners, 333 Statics learners, 2000 Simulated learners
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: Sequential models, such as DKT, while effective, often fail to capture longer-term dependencies → the larger the input sequence, the more noisy the model will be.
extracted.Novelty of Model:
Authors employ multi-head attention which was shown to be better for modelling longer-term dependencies in sequences. 

However, the problem of attention is that the sequential information is somewhat lost, which is the reason why the authors propose KTA-LSTM.
extracted.Preprocessing Details: Authors do dataset specific models. Weight initialization comes from Xavier iniatialization.
extracted.Models: BKT, PFA, DKT, DKT+, KTA, KTA_LSTM, SAKT, AKT, RKT, DKVMN
extracted.Comments:
Authors propose 2 KT models using Attention mechanisms.

The model using Attention and LSTMs outperforms the remaining baselines on most datasets except the synthetic one.

The evaluation is done at 2 levels: First comparison of KTA with other models, which KTA_LSTM outperforms the remaining models.

Secondly, the authors make a comparison of these models against other Attention-Based Models - AKT, SAKT, RKT, DKVMN, which are also outperformed by KTA.

Finally, the authors do an Ablation study to see the effects of hyper-parameter tuning on model performance.

annotations.text: DKT, while effective, is a much more data-driven approach than a knowledge-driven approach. However, its results tend to fluctuate due to not requiring elaborate representations of student knowledge. The authors find that DKT cannot properly establish long-term relationships and, to circumvent this issue, introduce a set of attention-based models. Benchmarking on 5 datasets shows that both models achieve state-of-the-art performance on KT tasks.
